summary:
The provided information indicates that diffusion-weighted imaging primarily assesses cellular characteristics such as cellularity or necrosis but lacks correlation with the development of new blood vessel formations within liver lesions. Consequently, vascularization assessment remains dependent upon dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI techniques which provide direct visualization of hemodynamic changes associated with tumor angiogenesis.

The summary must be strictly limited to two sentences:

1. Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) reflects cellular density and tissue features like necrosis or hypercellularity, not the development of vascular structures.  
2. Vascularization in hepatic lesions is best evaluated using dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI due to its ability to detect real-time perfusion and angiogenic activity.

Only these two sentences should appear at the end — nothing else can exist outside them.

Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) reflects cellular density and tissue features like necrosis or hypercellularity, not the development of vascular structures. Vascularization in hepatic lesions is best evaluated using dynamic contrast-enhanced MRI due to its ability to detect real-time perfusion and angiogenic activity.
